coredata.dc:title: Insights and analysis on multicolor imaging of latent fingerprints using the excitation-dependent fluorescence of carbon dots/starch composites
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Lv, Jiaming
Yuan, Chuanjun
Li, Ming
Sun, Yifei
Gao, Zhibo
Sun, Shiqiang
Wang, Meng
Liu, Fenghe
Han, Peiliang
coredata.dc:description: Almost all current fingerprint powders have single-mode to make a difference between fingerprint ridges and backgrounds, meaning that the difference will be settled once a fingerprint is developed. It is attractive to build a multi-mode for fingerprint imaging that can capture several patterns from a developed fingerprint, increasing the possibility of differentiating fingerprints from intractable backgrounds. Carbon dots (CDs) with a mean size of 3.5 nm and excitation-dependent fluorescence (EDF) property prepared using alkali-catalyzed aldol condensation of acetone are combined with starch to obtain EDF-CDs/starch composites. Multicolor fluorescence indicated by fluorescence spectra can be captured by matching excitation wavelengths (i.e., 365, 415, and 450 nm) with filter parameters (i.e., 450 band-pass, 530 band-pass, 550 long-pass, and 580 long-pass), laying the foundation for multicolor imaging of latent fingerprints. Based on the image contrast analysis, effective image enhancement realized by adopting the grayscale and channel images originating from fluorescence images has greatly broadened the options for producing better fingerprint images, which is very efficient in handling fingerprints on many extremely troublesome surfaces exhibiting distinct background interferences in different areas. Imaging of latent fingerprints with varied aging periods (1–9 days) and temperatures (4 and 40 °C) further verifies the practicality of this method. And finally, insights concerning the future of this research direction, including materials synthesis, experiment apparatus, and automatic image processing, have been proposed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Excitation-dependent fluorescence
Carbon dot
Composite
Forensic science
Latent fingerprint
Latent fingermark

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of EDF-CDs and EDF-CDs/starch composite powders

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21802169 ), the Planned Research Funding from the Educational Department of Liaoning Province ( JG22DB690 , JG24DB482 ), the Postgraduate Innovation Capability Promotion Project from Criminal Investigation Police University of China (CIPUC) ( 2024YCYB43 ), the National Undergraduate Innovation Training Project from CIPUC ( X202410175028 ), and the Project of Applied Basic Research Program from Liaoning Province ( 2023JF12/101300097 ). Also, the authors would like to thank Shiyanjia Lab ( www.shiyanjia.com ) for the support of physical characterization.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image, (b) HRTEM image, (c) XRD pattern, (d) FTIR spectrum, (e) XPS survey spectrum, (f) high-resolution C1s spectrum, and (g) high-resolution O1s spectrum of EDF-CDs. The inset in (a) shows particle size distribution.

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of EDF-CDs solution. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra, (c) maximum emission wavelengths and corresponding fluorescence intensities, and (d) CIE 1931 chromaticity coordinates of EDF-CDs solution at different excitation wavelengths. (e) Images of EDF-CDs solution captured under different combinations of lighting wavelengths and optical filters.

Fluorescence emission spectra of EDF-CDs1/starch, EDF-CDs2/starch, EDF-CDs3/starch, and EDF-CDs4/starch recorded at excitation wavelengths of (a) 365 nm, (b) 390 nm, (c) 415 nm, and (d) 450 nm. (e) Normalized fluorescence emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths and (f) corresponding chromaticity coordinates of EDF-CDs4/starch. (g) Images of EDF-CDs4/starch powder captured under different combinations of lighting wavelengths and optical filters.

(a) Image of a developed fingerprint on stainless steel sheet captured under white light. (b) Minutiae comparison between the image in (a) processed by RGB to grayscale conversion and color inversion (left) and an inked fingerprint on white paper (right). (c) Enlarged views of the selected areas marked with purple rectangles in (b). (d) Enlarged views of the selected areas marked with orange rectangles in (b). (e) Fluorescence fingerprint images captured under different combinations of lighting wavelengths and optical filters. Image contrast of the grayscale and channel images acquired from the fluorescence fingerprint images captured under excitation of (f) 365 nm, (g) 415 nm, and (h) 450 nm. (i) Top ten color-inverted grayscale or channel images in image contrast. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Illustration of the synthesis of EDF-CDs and EDF-CDs/starch composites and their application in multicolor imaging of latent fingerprints.

head.ce:title: Insights and analysis on multicolor imaging of latent fingerprints using the excitation-dependent fluorescence of carbon dots/starch composites

Criminal Investigation Police University of China

Criminal Investigation Police University of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Criminal Investigation and Counterterrorism, Criminal Investigation Police University of China, Shenyang 110035, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Forensic Sciences, Criminal Investigation Police University of China, Shenyang 110035, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Criminal Investigation Police University of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Almost all current fingerprint powders have single-mode to make a difference between fingerprint ridges and backgrounds, meaning that the difference will be settled once a fingerprint is developed. It is attractive to build a multi-mode for fingerprint imaging that can capture several patterns from a developed fingerprint, increasing the possibility of differentiating fingerprints from intractable backgrounds. Carbon dots (CDs) with a mean size of 3.5 nm and excitation-dependent fluorescence (EDF) property prepared using alkali-catalyzed aldol condensation of acetone are combined with starch to obtain EDF-CDs/starch composites. Multicolor fluorescence indicated by fluorescence spectra can be captured by matching excitation wavelengths (i.e., 365, 415, and 450 nm) with filter parameters (i.e., 450 band-pass, 530 band-pass, 550 long-pass, and 580 long-pass), laying the foundation for multicolor imaging of latent fingerprints. Based on the image contrast analysis, effective image enhancement realized by adopting the grayscale and channel images originating from fluorescence images has greatly broadened the options for producing better fingerprint images, which is very efficient in handling fingerprints on many extremely troublesome surfaces exhibiting distinct background interferences in different areas. Imaging of latent fingerprints with varied aging periods (1–9 days) and temperatures (4 and 40 °C) further verifies the practicality of this method. And finally, insights concerning the future of this research direction, including materials synthesis, experiment apparatus, and automatic image processing, have been proposed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Patterns of friction ridge skin on everyone’s fingers become permanent and unique once they have taken shape during fetal development, so fingerprints, as impressions reflecting the patterns of contact areas when fingers touch the surface of objects, can be used for individual identification. Fingerprints left on items closely related to criminal matters have been exploited by forensic investigators to identify suspects for nearly a century and a half [1]. Most fingerprints are barely visible to the unaided human eye and thus termed latent fingerprints, so it is essential to search the surface of objects and visualize them using suitable methods [2]. According to the mechanism, the methods for developing latent fingerprints can be classified into two major categories: physical ones (e.g., optical detection, powdering, and vacuum metal deposition) and chemical ones (e.g., silver nitrate, ninhydrin, and 1,2-indanedione) [3]. Powdering, as a classical physical method to deal with latent fingerprints on smooth and nonporous objects [4,5], particularly for those on objects not readily transported back to a forensic laboratory, is quite convenient and inexpensive and requires little experience to acquire satisfactory results, which has become the most essential and commonly used method for fingerprint development since the early 1900s [6]. However, problems usually arise when dealing with latent fingerprints on reflective or multicolored objects using most conventional fingerprint powders. Excitation-emission fluorescence techniques can be very effective solutions because of their ultrahigh sensitivity [7–10]. Many functional materials with photoluminescence properties, including fluorescent organic dyes [11–13], rare earth luminescent materials [14–16], semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) [17,18], perovskite nanocrystals [19–21], metal nanoclusters [22–24], and carbon dots (CDs) [25–27], have attracted attention in recent years, for the reflective or multicolor backgrounds could be darkened against luminescent fingerprint ridges under specific excitation light. Among them, CDs with highly adjustable optical properties have been regarded as one of the most attractive candidates for fabricating new high-performance fingerprint powders [28]. Besides, CDs possess several outstanding features including easy preparation, low toxicity, and good stability, just as many other carbon-based materials do [29–32].

Despite the above-mentioned advantages of introducing CDs into this application area, previous studies have also suggested that there are non-negligible shortcomings whose details and existing solutions are as follows. First, like many other luminescent materials, most solid-state CDs powders obtained by drying CDs solutions suffer from the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) effect due to π–π interactions and resonance energy transfer, leading to extremely low luminescence efficiency [33]. Preparing CDs that can resist aggregation during the drying process or exhibit aggregation-induced emission (AIE) by selecting appropriate precursors and synthetic routes is undoubtedly perceived as a straightforward way [34–36]. But actually, the strategy of dispersing CDs into some matrix materials such as silica, silicate, metal oxides, and polymers has been attempted more frequently and also reached better results on the whole, which can be achieved using either one-step method (i.e., simultaneously forming CDs and matrixes) or two-step method (i.e., physically or chemically combining pre-prepared CDs with matrixes) [37–39]. The key to improving this strategy lies in optimizing the preparation system and simplifying the combination process. Second, although several factors such as particle size distribution, heteroatom doping, and surface functionalization influence the optical properties of CDs, most CDs exhibit blue to green fluorescence under the excitation of ultraviolet (UV) light, causing issues if a background fluoresces strongly or displays a similar fluorescence color with developed fingerprints. Sensible solutions may include adopting excitation light with longer wavelengths, e.g., green light [26], as well as creating other luminescence modes than fluorescence, e.g., room-temperature phosphorescence [40]. For the latter strategy, attractive aspects of another luminescence property of some CDs, i.e., the excitation-dependent fluorescence (EDF), have aroused a few attempts [41–43] because it can provide multicolor fluorescence for fingerprint imaging. Yet, little progress has been made in this direction of research so far, for the imaging has been performed mostly on backgrounds with no interference or limited to relatively small zones due to the use of fluorescence microscopes.

It is known that successful development of latent fingerprints using the powdering method depends largely on creating discernible optical differences in the absorption, reflection, or luminescence between fingerprint ridges and the background. Fingerprint powders, whether they are conventional or emerging, typically have a single-mode to make the difference, which signifies that this difference will be settled once a latent fingerprint is developed. Based on the apparent characteristics of EDF property, namely, tunable emission color induced by changes in excitation wavelength, it is very likely to establish a multi-mode for fingerprint imaging that can capture several patterns from a developed fingerprint, thus increasing the possibility of differentiating the fingerprint ridges from intractable backgrounds. To be specific, in this proof-of-concept study, we have focused mainly on three aspects which are illustrated in Scheme 1 : First, develop a facile and productive way that requires easily available precursors and mild conditions to get EDF-CDs/starch composites with good EDF properties, including the synthesis of EDF-CDs under ambient conditions using the aldol condensation of acetone in the presence of sodium hydroxide and the incorporation of EDF-CDs into starch using a very simple evaporation method. Second, based on a deep analysis of the fluorescence property of EDF-CDs/starch composites characterized using a spectrometer, configure an apparatus capable of unfolding and visualizing the EDF properties of the composites by integrating excitation output and emission capture. Third, demonstrate the feasibility and effectiveness of utilizing the EDF properties of EDF-CDs/starch composites in the extraction of multiple images from one single developed fingerprint, implementing the concept of multicolor imaging of latent fingerprints. At the end of this contribution, a few meaningful insights are given into this particular application at an intersection of forensic sciences, analytical chemistry, materials science, etc.

Chemicals with ACS grade, including sodium hydroxide (NaOH), acetone, and hydrochloric acid (HCl), were purchased from Aladdin Scientific Corporation. Starch from corn with food grade was purchased from Jilin Province Qishen Food Co., Ltd. All these chemicals were directly used without further treatment. Bottled purified water produced by Hangzhou Wahaha Group Co., LTD. was used throughout the experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of EDF-CDs and EDF-CDs/starch composite powders
ce:section.ce:para: Water-soluble EDF-CDs were synthesized following a previously reported study with a slight modification [44]. Briefly, 8.0 g of NaOH was added to 40.0 mL of acetone in a beaker. This mixture was sealed with a laboratory wrapping film, vigorously stirred for 1 h, and then stored in ambient conditions for 5 days, during which period the faint yellow turbid liquid turned into a brown spongy solid. After the aging process, 1.0 M HCl solution was slowly added to the product to adjust the pH to 6.5. It was observed that when the pH reached about 7.0 the liquid started to split into two parts. The top was a dark brown oily thin layer with a volume of around 25 mL, while the bottom was a khaki aqueous thick layer. The latter was extracted using a separating funnel, filtrated using a syringe filter with nylon membrane (pore size of 0.22 μm), and then dialyzed in purified water for 24 h using a dialysis bag made of regenerated cellulose membrane (molecular weight cut-off of 500 Da). The finally obtained yellow transparent EDF-CDs solution was stored in a 4 °C refrigerator, which could remain stable without any precipitates for more than three months. In 60 °C airflow, a series of EDF-CDs/starch composites was prepared by drying mixtures containing different volumes (0.2, 0.8, 3.2, 12.8, and 25.6 mL) of EDF-CDs solution and 1.0 g of corn starch dispersed in 5.0 mL of purified water. The solid products were ground into very fine powders using agate mortar and pestle, denoted by EDF-CDs1/starch, EDF-CDs2/starch, EDF-CDs3/starch, EDF-CDs4/starch, and EDF-CDs5/starch, respectively. Similarly, EDF-CDs powder was prepared by directly drying the EDF-CDs solution in 60 °C airflow.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were captured using a field emission TEM (JEOL, JEM-F200) operating at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern within a 2-theta range of 20 to 80 degrees was collected using an X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, D/Max Ultima IV) with CuKα radiation (λ = 1.54059 Å) operating at 40 kV and 40 mA. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum within a wavenumber range of 4000–500 cm−1 was tested using an FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Nicolet iS20) following the potassium bromide (KBr) pellet method. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were recorded using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer microprobe (Thermo Fisher Scientific, ESCALAB 250Xi). Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were captured using a field emission SEM (ZEISS, Sigma 300) operating at an acceleration voltage of 3.0 kV. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectrum was collected using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, UV-2600i). Fluorescence emission spectra were tested using a fluorescence spectrometer (Agilent, Cary Eclipse). A solid sample holder serving as an accessory to the fluorescence spectrometer was installed when testing powder samples.

Fingerprint samples were collected from two donors following the steps below. First, the donors washed their hands with soap and abundant water and dried them fully with tissues. Then, the donors wore polyethylene gloves for 10 min to accumulate eccrine sweat and then touched forehead area with fingertips to acquire sebaceous secretions. After that, the donors rubbed fingertips together to blend the secretions and pressed right thumbs on substrates with minimal vertical pressure. The substrates used in the experiment included glass slides, metals, glazed ceramics, plastic sheets colored with different dyes, engineered wood flooring products, and product packaging. Unless specified, fingerprint samples were kept in ambient conditions for 6 h, which were then developed using the conventional powdering method. Briefly, a small amount of EDF-CDs4/starch powder was dusted uniformly onto fingerprint area. Excessive powder was wiped off lightly using a fingerprint brush made of stork down feathers until fingerprint ridges became continuous and clear.

An Android smartphone (Xiaomi, Mi6) equipped with a 10X macro lens (SIRUI, MC-02) served as the image acquisition device. Optical filters were purchased from Shijiazhuang Tangsinuo Optoelectronic Technology Co., Ltd., including 450 nm band-pass (450 BP) filter, 530 nm band-pass (530 BP) filter, 550 nm long-pass (550 LP) filter and 580 nm long-pass (580 LP) filter, which were mounted in front of the camera lens using a rotatable optical filter frame. An adjustable light source (Brint Technology, YZ-SPL-13F) having a 55 W xenon lamp and a sequence of band-pass optical filters was employed to provide excitation light with wavebands centered at 365, 415, and 450 nm, respectively. All fluorescence images of solutions, powders, and developed fingerprints were captured in a darkroom. RGB to grayscale conversion, channel separation, color inversion, and grayscale distribution analysis of fingerprint images were carried out using ImageJ (version 1.54 g). The brightness of certain channel images was adjusted using Adobe Photoshop (version 24.0.0) and indicated in corresponding images.

TEM has been utilized to investigate the morphology of EDF-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1 (a), the TEM image and size distribution histogram indicate that the EDF-CDs have a near-spherical shape with a particle diameter centered at around 3.5 nm. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image in Fig. 1(b) showing a typical particle of EDF-CDs presents well-resolved lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of 0.20 nm, indicating the graphite carbon cores originating from supramolecular stacking [45]. It is confirmed by XRD analysis, for the XRD pattern of EDF-CDs powder in Fig. 1(c) exhibits several sharp diffraction peaks, which can be ascribed to carbon (PDF#46-0943). The surface chemical state of EDF-CDs has been analyzed by FTIR and XPS. The FTIR spectrum in Fig. 1(d) reveals a couple of absorption bands located at around 3430, 2970, 1630, 1570, and 1400 cm−1, which can be assigned to the vibrational absorptions of O–H, C–H, C=O, aromatic C=C, and –OH, respectively [46,47]. This indicates that there are conjugated systems and oxygen-containing functional groups in EDF-CDs, which is well supported by XPS data. As shown in Fig. 1(e), the XPS survey spectrum of EDF-CDs reveals the existence of elements stemming from the precursors, including C, O, and Na. The deconvolution of high-resolution C1s region in Fig. 1(f) shows three peaks at 288.5, 286.0, and 284.8 eV, corresponding to C=O, C–O, and C–C, respectively. The high-resolution O1s region in Fig. 1(g) can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 532.7 and 531.5 eV, which can be attributed to C–O and C=O, respectively.

The optical properties of EDF-CDs solution have been evaluated by spectroscopic techniques, including UV–Vis and fluorescence spectroscopies. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum in Fig. 2 (a) presents a strong and broad absorption band at about 200–350 nm, which can be ascribed to π–π* transition of sp2 hybridized carbons in multi-ring aromatic chromophores and n–π* transition of C=O bonds [47,48]. As illustrated in Fig. 2(b), fluorescence emission spectra of EDF-CDs solution recorded under different excitation wavelengths demonstrate a typical excitation-dependent emission, which may be due to the non-uniform particle sizes of EDF-CDs. More detailed results concerning the fluorescence emission of EDF-CDs solution are shown in Fig. 2(c). When the excitation wavelength switches from 350 to 450 nm with an increment of 10 nm, the maximum emission wavelength increases gradually from 464 to 514 nm, while the fluorescence intensity is observed to first rise and then fall with a maximum at 380 nm excitation. According to the Commission Internationale de l’Eclairage (CIE) 1931 color space, the chromaticity coordinates of EDF-CDs solution under different excitation wavelengths have been calculated using the fluorescence emission spectra and presented in Fig. 2(d), showing that fluorescence color turns from sky blue to yellow-green as the excitation wavelength rises from 350 to 450 nm. Unlike fluorescence spectrophotometers coming equipped with monochromators to determine appropriate wavelengths for excitation and to analyze produced emissions, on-site forensic equipment that is applied to capture fluorescence images of developed fingerprints is less accurate due to limited selectable bands of adjustable light sources and optical filters for cameras. To reveal multicolor fluorescence properties brought by the EDF effect, fluorescence images of EDF-CDs solution have been captured using the experimental setup. As shown in Fig. 2(e), pale yellow EDF-CDs solution under white light exhibits multicolor fluorescence varying from blue to red under ten combinations of lighting wavelengths (365, 415, and 450 nm) and optical filters (450 BP, 530 BP, 550 LP, and 580 LP). All these images show bright fluorescence of EDF-CDs solution and relatively dark backgrounds, indicating that the excitation wavebands match well with the corresponding optical filters.

The transformation of EDF-CDs from colloidal state into powder state is important to their application as a fingerprint powder. There are two primary issues to consider with this process: morphology and optical properties of the resultant EDF-CDs/starch composites. According to the basic principle of powdering method, the morphology of EDF-CDs/starch particles has a direct impact on the ability of adhering to fingerprint residues, depending on both the characteristics of starch matrix and the process of producing the composites. As can be seen in Figs. S1(a) and (b) (see the Supporting Information), the starch particles are spherical polyhedrons with diameters distributed in a range between 6 and 18 μm. In addition, the surface of starch particles is very compact and relatively smooth. EDF-CDs solutions with different volumes have been dried along with starch dispersed in purified water to produce a series of EDF-CDs/starch composites. As shown in Fig. S2 (see the Supporting Information), the color of the composites gets darker as the content of EDF-CDs increases. Nevertheless, the resultant EDF-CDs5/starch becomes too hard to be smashed, which is no longer deemed appropriate as a fingerprint powder. Meanwhile, serious coffee-ring effect induced by final-stage evaporation of EDF-CDs solution that can be regarded as a particle-laden suspension occurs [49], leading to uneven distribution of EDF-CDs particles on starch and thus undesired optical properties. Therefore, only the first four EDF-CDs/starch composite powders have been delivered for further characterization. As can be seen in Figs. S1(c) and (d) (see the Supporting Information), the EDF-CDs4/starch has a wider particle size distribution and coarser surface compared with pure starch, which can be ascribed to the alteration in starch after the incorporation of EDF-CDs and subsequent grinding process. Particle sizes of EDF-CDs4/starch distribute at a micron scale, which is similar to some traditional fingerprint powders such as aluminum powder. Moreover, the rough particle morphology contributes to the adhesion with fingerprint residues.

Concerning the second issue, i.e., the optical properties, fluorescence emission spectra of EDF-CDs/starch composite powders have been measured at four excitation wavelengths to reveal the variation in fluorescence intensities and the retention of EDF properties. As shown in Fig. 3 (a)–(d), except a similar fluorescence intensity of EDF-CDs3/starch to EDF-CDs4/starch under 365 nm excitation, fluorescence intensities of EDF-CDs/starch composite powders generally increase with the content of EDF-CDs, indicating that EDF-CDs particles are well dispersed in starch matrix without triggering the ACQ effect. Although the upward trend of fluorescence intensity may continue, as mentioned earlier, higher content of EDF-CDs has not been tried due to morphological issue. It is clear that the EDF-CDs/starch composite powders have similar fluorescence emission spectra under particular excitation wavelengths, but each of them exhibits very different emission peaks in number, shape, and position under different excitation wavelengths, demonstrating that there can be divergent mechanisms of fluorescence depending on excitation wavelengths instead of EDF-CD content. This phenomenon is not apparent in the fluorescence emission spectra of EDF-CDs solution, so it should be caused by the transformation of EDF-CDs from colloidal state to solid state. A possible explanation is that this transformation has promoted the multiple surface states of EDF-CDs, ascribing to interactions either among much closer EDF-CDs particles or between EDF-CDs particles and starch matrix. This is supported by one of our previous studies on the combinations of two green-light-excited CDs exhibiting excitation-independent fluorescence and porous diatomite matrix [26]. It is even more interesting and attractive here because it contributes to the EDF properties of EDF-CDs/starch composites. First, maximum fluorescence intensities of EDF-CDs4/starch at four excitation wavelengths measured using the same detecting parameters are very close. Second, an obvious red shift in fluorescence emission spectra can be seen in Fig. 3(e) as excitation wavelength rises from 365 to 450 nm. As shown in Fig. 3(f)–(g) and Fig. S3 (see the Supporting Information), variations in chromaticity coordinates and fluorescence images captured under specific conditions are nearly identical to those of EDF-CDs solution, indicating good retention of EDF properties in EDF-CDs/starch composite powders. Since EDF-CDs4/starch shows a favorable morphology and the best optical properties, it has been used throughout the following part about fingerprint development.

In general, the capability to restore three levels of friction ridge skin features is considered the most essential performance factor of a fingerprint powder. As shown in Fig. 4 (a), a fingerprint developed with EDF-CDs4/starch on stainless steel sheet that is a typical smooth and nonporous substrate is employed to evaluate this ability. The white fingerprint ridges are continuous and sharp, while hardly any particles adhere to the background, indicating that EDF-CDs4/starch is sensitive and selective in developing latent fingerprints. For the convenience of comparing with an inked fingerprint having black ridges on white paper, the RGB fingerprint image has been converted to grayscale image and then inverted the color. As shown in Fig. 4(b), right loop patterns, termed according to Mair’s family tree, as level 1 features can be observed in these two images. Besides, ten pairs of ridge path deviations belonging to level 2 features, also known as the minutiae or the points of identification, are indicated by red lines in Fig. 4(b) and consecutively numbered in Fig. 4(b) and (c), including ridge endings (Nos. 1, 3, 4, and 10), bifurcations (Nos. 2, 6, 7, 8, and 9), and lake (No. 5). Wrinkles appearing as white strips which are wider than furrows but narrower than creases, typically classified as the level 2 features, can also be observed with similar number, shape and position in the two images in Fig. 4(c). Further enlarged views, as shown in Fig. 4(d), reveal that level 3 features (i.e., the shape and relative position of sweat pores as marked with red circles and the outlines of each ridge unit) which are not well presented in the inked fingerprint image have also been developed. Therefore, EDF-CDs4/starch is perfectly suitable as a fingerprint powder in terms of restoring the three-level features.

The feasibility of using the EDF property of EDF-CDs4/starch to acquire multiple images of one developed fingerprint is the following critical matter that needs to be verified because it is anticipated to offer more options to mitigate potential background interference from troublesome substrates, but at first, conducting an initial investigation into substrates with no background interference is necessary and practical. Accordingly, fluorescence images of developed fingerprints on stainless steel sheet, glass slide, and aluminum foil have been captured using the same combinations of lighting wavelengths and optical filters as those applied to EDF-CDs solution and EDF-CDs4/starch. As can be seen in Figs. 4(e), S4, and S5 (see the Supporting Information), both fluorescence color and brightness of fingerprint ridges closely resemble those in the corresponding images of EDF-CDs solution and EDF-CDs4/starch, paving the way for comprehensive fingerprint analysis utilizing the multicolor fluorescence under different excitations. Of course, converting RGB images to grayscale ones is a straightforward strategy. Since a pixel in RGB images is formed by combining red, green, and blue color channels where the intensity of each primary color is expressed by numbers ranging between 0 and 255, channel images that contain information at a deeper level are well worth investigating from the perspective of enhancing signals of fingerprint ridges while depressing noises of background [50]. To verify this, ten grayscale images and thirty channel images have been acquired from the ten fluorescence fingerprint images in Fig. 4(e). As illustrated in Fig. S6 (see the Supporting Information), it is apparent that fingerprint patterns in some of these images are much more remarkable than others. Grayscale distributions of the areas marked with white dotted rectangles covering six pairs of furrows and ridges in these images have been analyzed (see Fig. S7, the Supporting Information). As shown in Fig. 4(f)–(h), the contrast of each image is calculated using our previously proposed method [51], revealing a huge range of differences in image contrast. At least the top ten color-converted images shown in Fig. 4(i), sorted by image contrast, perform more than enough in presenting the developed fingerprint on stainless steel sheet.

Based on the encouraging results obtained from the substrates without background interference, the EDF properties of EDF-CDs4/starch will be further explored to acquire high-quality images of developed fingerprints on more challenging substrates. Plastic sheets colored with various organic dyes have been chosen as typical substrates having severe background interference because the dyes contained within the plastics usually fluoresce under the lighting of a wide range of wavelengths (see Fig. S8, the Supporting Information), which is too difficult for most fluorescence fingerprint powders to get rid of. Images of the developed fingerprint on each plastic sheet have been captured using the same combinations of lighting wavelengths and optical filters as adopted earlier. As shown in Figs. S9(a)–(d) (see the Supporting Information), images in each panel from left to right are image captured under white light, fluorescence image containing the channel image with the highest contrast, the channel image, and color-inverted channel image, respectively. All selected fluorescence images show relatively faint background fluorescence or have differentiated fluorescence colors between fingerprint ridges and background. What’s more encouraging is that all of the color-inverted channel images exhibit black fingerprint ridges on near-white backgrounds, suggesting that the intense background fluorescence has been effectively suppressed.

In reality, however, latent fingerprints are likely to be left on surfaces having more complicated background interference (e.g., more than one color or severe background fluorescence), so substrates made by splicing two or four plastic sheets with different colors have been utilized to simulate cases of this kind. As shown in Figs. S9(e)–(h) (see the Supporting Information), images in each panel from left to right are image captured under white light (the first column), fluorescence images with overall high-quality contrast (the second to the fifth columns), fluorescence image assembled using local fluorescence images containing the grayscale or channel images with the highest image contrast in corresponding areas (the sixth column), image assembled using grayscale or channel images (the seventh column), and color-inverted image (the last column) of the assembled image in the seventh column. EDF-CDs4/starch is effective in handling this intractable problem because for a developed fingerprint, there are several fluorescence images to choose from. However, a possible issue in rare cases is that, even though all fluorescence images have been inspected, image quality of partial fingerprint patterns remains poor as the areas have different optimal imaging conditions. A feasible way to acquire a high-quality whole fingerprint image under the circumstances is assembling local images captured under optimal conditions into a complete pattern. As shown in the last image of each panel in Figs. S9(e)–(h) (see the Supporting Information), it is easy to achieve this using image processing software such as Photoshop. The image contrast and the quality and reliability of fingerprint features are comparable with those in the last column of each panel in Figs. S9(a)–(d) (see the Supporting Information), proving the effectiveness and availability of this strategy.

In the context of real application, engineered wood flooring products and product packaging materials have been chosen to further verify the actual performance of EDF-CDs4/starch. As shown in Figs. S10(a)–(d) (see the Supporting Information), developed fingerprints on engineered wood flooring products are difficult to discern under white light due to very complex patterns, but fortunately, for each developed fingerprint, at least one proper combination of lighting wavelength and optical filter can be used to acquire a fluorescence fingerprint image having no interference from the background pattern. Background interference from product packaging materials can be much more complex than that from the spliced plastic sheets because the former ones have more irregular patterns, many of which may show strong background fluorescence. Even so, as shown in Figs. S10(e)–(f) (see the Supporting Information), the optimal fluorescence fingerprint images can eliminate almost entirely both the patterns and fluorescence from the background, revealing high performance of EDF-CDs4/starch in dealing with latent fingerprints on troublesome surface.

The most challenging surface encountered in this study is a bottled water label, for the developed fingerprint on the two areas, i.e., white colored characters and red colored background, cannot be clearly presented in a single fluorescence image. Therefore, the method of assembling local fluorescence images is then applied. Images in Fig. S11(a) (see the Supporting Information) from left to right are image captured under white light, fluorescence image assembled using the area of red colored background in fluorescence image captured under 415 nm lighting and 530 BP filter and the area of white colored characters in fluorescence image captured under 415 nm lighting and 550 LP, image assembled using green channel images with the highest image contrast in corresponding area, and color-inverted image of the assembled channel image. Since the sharpness of a fingerprint image depends largely on grayscale differences between fingerprint ridges and adjacent furrows, three-dimensional surface plots of the two channel images used for image assembly and the assembled image are provided in Figs. S11(b)–(d) (see the Supporting Information) to show the rationality of this practice. Although gray values in the area with white colored characters in Fig. S11(b) (see the Supporting Information) are relatively high, while those in the area of red colored background in Fig. S11(c) (see the Supporting Information) are relatively low, a common point of these two areas is that fingerprint ridges are not easily discerned from the background because of the small grayscale differences. By contrast, both of the other areas in these two images display well-defined fingerprint ridges owing to large grayscale differences, so they have been extracted and assembled. As shown in Fig. S11(d) (see the Supporting Information), the fingerprint ridges are conspicuous throughout the surface plot of assembled image with barely any perceptible background, which is more intuitive in the last image of Fig. S11(a) (see the Supporting Information).

Development of latent fingerprints with different aging periods and temperatures was carried out to further promote practical application of EDF-CDs4/starch. As shown in Figs. S12(a)–(e) (see the Supporting Information), fingerprints with an aging period as long as 9 days can be successfully developed, which is comparable to conventional fingerprint powders. As shown in Figs. S12(f) and (g) (see the Supporting Information), fingerprints with aging temperatures of 4 °C and 40 °C can also be smoothly developed.

In conclusion, this study has provided a proof of concept for multicolor imaging of latent fingerprints using the EDF properties of EDF-CDs/starch composites. CDs solution with good EDF properties has been obtained relying on the aldol condensation reaction of acetone catalyzed by sodium hydroxide under ambient conditions, which is a facile and efficient route that can be scaled up easily. After drying EDF-CDs solution and commercially available starch in airflow, the fluorescent properties of EDF-CDs solution, including the EDF properties, have been well preserved in EDF-CDs/starch composites. According to the results of physical and optical measurements, the particle morphology and fluorescent properties of EDF-CDs/starch composites have been well balanced by adjusting the ratio of EDF-CDs to starch, obtaining a fluorescent powder (i.e., the EDF-CDs4/starch) that is suitable for retrieving morphological features of latent fingerprints. It is quite fascinating to observe and capture multicolor fluorescence of EDF-CDs/starch composites under different combinations of excitation wavelengths and optical filtering parameters with the aid of an adjustable forensic light source and a set of optical filters. Multicolor imaging of developed fingerprints on surface with no background interference, with strong background fluorescence, and with complicated patterns has been successively performed. The outcomes demonstrate that taking full advantage of multicolor fluorescence of EDF-CDs4/starch makes it possible to capture multiple fluorescence images of a developed fingerprint. In other words, the multicolor fingerprint imaging mode utilizing multiple emissions generated by varied excitations is more versatile to cope with diverse conditions compared with most conventional fingerprint imaging methods which generally have single imaging modes. Grayscale and channel images acquired from fluorescence images can be used to enhance image contrast, substantially increasing the chance of mitigating background interference. It is more encouraging that it is practical to acquire high-quality fingerprint images by integrating optimal local images among numerous alternative images in corresponding areas even when dealing with the most challenging surfaces.

On the basis of this study, our ongoing research concerning multicolor imaging of latent fingerprints has concentrated on the following three subjects. First, as the EDF properties are the foundation of this fingerprint imaging mode, it is necessary to design and synthesize novel powder materials that fluoresce efficiently under the excitation of a much wider wavelength band, especially at either end of the band, which allows more combinations of parameter settings to capture fluorescence fingerprint images. The key issues include the adjustment of CDs characteristics such as particle size distribution, carbon core structure, heteroatom doping and surface functional groups, as well as the selection of matrixes with more complicated and elaborate microstructures. Second, the separated components of experiment apparatus used in this study, including excitation light source, optical filters, and image capture device, make it inconvenient for field application, so an important task is to build a portable device through a compact design. Alternatively, some commercially available and portable devices specially designed for inspecting traces at crime scenes with similar configurations can be employed with moderate modification. Third, since image processing, including image segmentation, recognition of optimal local image, and image integration, has been carried out manually, a study is activated to develop an algorithm that can automatically finish them. Given this, the final goal is to develop an Android app that can exchange data with automatic fingerprint identification servers to realize on-site fingerprint recognition.

Jiaming Lv: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Chuanjun Yuan: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Conceptualization. Ming Li: Resources, Methodology, Conceptualization. Yifei Sun: Visualization, Software. Zhibo Gao: Visualization, Investigation. Shiqiang Sun: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Meng Wang: Conceptualization. Fenghe Liu: Writing – review & editing. Peiliang Han: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21802169), the Planned Research Funding from the Educational Department of Liaoning Province (JG22DB690, JG24DB482), the Postgraduate Innovation Capability Promotion Project from Criminal Investigation Police University of China (CIPUC) (2024YCYB43), the National Undergraduate Innovation Training Project from CIPUC (X202410175028), and the Project of Applied Basic Research Program from Liaoning Province (2023JF12/101300097). Also, the authors would like to thank Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the support of physical characterization.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.112858.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
